title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 Academy (Part 1)
text:
Program Review 2020-2021: Law Enforcement Academy
Prepared by: Abner Weed
1. Description of Program:
The Law Enforcement Academy state mandated curriculum which prepares students for
law enforcement careers in a variety of criminal justice fields at the local, state, and
national levels. The program is designed to instruct students on the principles of the
federal, state, and local justice systems and subsystems. Emphasis is placed on
practical exposure to Crime Scene Investigation, Criminal Law, Criminal Investigations,
Forensics, Emergency Management, Homeland Security, Principles of Criminal
Defense, Community Relations, Corrections, Firearms Training, Juvenile Law, Arrest
and Control, Emergency Vehicle Operations, and California Laws. The academy
program at the college dovetails into an Associate of Arts degree or an Associate in
Science degree for transfer to a California State University. The provides required
perishable skills and advanced officer training courses for the Law Enforcement
Community.
a) Organization: The ADJ Program is under direction of the Dean of Career and
Technical Education. The ADJ faculty include one full-time Coordinator of the
Law Enforcement Academy, several adjunct faculty teaching in the Academy,
and one full-time faculty teaching the degree curriculum.
b) Primary purpose: The primary purpose of the program is to prepare students for
careers in criminal justice fields.
c) Whom you serve: We serve all students interested in criminal justice careers
and provide curriculum to assist those already in the law enforcement fields to
obtain their degree and maintain their perishable skills.
d) Services provided: We offer a modular format Law Enforcement Academy and
curriculum leading to degrees and a Certificate of Achievement.
e) How services are provided:
a. Face to face Academy
b. Degree applicable coursework
c. Transfer courses
d. Vocational courses
f) Describe how curriculum is up to date and needs based: Curriculum for the
Law Enforcement Academy is reviewed and updated by P.O.S.T. (Peace Officer
Standards and Training) as needed. Those changes are reflected in our
mandated courses and approved by P.O.S.T. before our academy starts. The
Curriculum for the degree and COA are updated every two years, as required.
The most recent updates were completed December 4, 2020. The curriculum is

-- 1 of 8 --

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 Academy (Part 2)
text:
aligned with the ADJ Programs of several north-state community colleges, and
fulfills the needs of those interested in careers in the criminal justice system.
g) Breakdown of classes offered:
In July 1993, the Commission adopted training specifications as its method of
specifying the minimum, mandated curriculum for the Regular Basic Course. Listed
below are the individual learning domains that make up the course curriculum.
• LD 01 Leadership, Professionalism and Ethics
• LD 02 Criminal Justice System
• LD 03 Principled Policing in the Community
• LD 04 Victimology/Crisis Intervention
• LD 05 Introduction to Criminal Law
• LD 06 Property Crimes
• LD 07 Crimes Against Persons
• LD 08 General Criminal Statutes
• LD 09 Crimes Against Children
• LD 10 Sex Crimes
• LD 11 Juvenile Law and Procedure
• LD 12 Controlled Substances
• LD 13 ABC Law
• LD 15 Laws of Arrest
• LD 16 Search and Seizure
• LD 17 Presentation of Evidence
• LD 18 Investigative Report Writing
• LD 19 Vehicle Operations
• LD 20 Use of Force/De-escalation
• LD 21 Patrol Techniques
• LD 22 Vehicle Pullovers
• LD 23 Crimes in Progress
• LD 24 Handling Disputes/Crowd Control
• LD 25 Domestic Violence
• LD 26 Critical Incidents
• LD 27 Missing Persons
• LD 28 Traffic Enforcement
• LD 29 Traffic Accident Investigation
• LD 30 Crime Scenes, Evidence, and Forensics
• LD 31 Custody
• LD 32 Lifetime Fitness

-- 2 of 8 --

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 Academy (Part 3)
text:
• LD 33 Arrest and Control
• LD 34 First Aid and CPR (doc)
• LD 35 Firearms/Chemical Agents (doc)
• LD 36 Information Systems (doc)
• LD 37 People with Disabilities (doc)
• LD 38 Gang Awareness (doc)
• LD 39 Crimes Against the Justice System (doc)
• LD 40 Weapons Violations (doc)
• LD 42 Cultural Diversity/Discrimination (doc)
• LD 43 Terrorism Awareness (doc)
2. External factors impacting program
a) Budgetary constraints or opportunities: Inadequate budget development relating to
the Police Academy; inadequate facilities for future program growth
b) Competition from other colleges; Nearest law enforcement academies do not
compete with our police academy (e.g., Butte, Redwoods, Yuba, Sacramento, etc.)
Degree programs – online offerings dissipate direct competition from other
institutions.
a) University requirements: None.
b) Institutional regulation, policies, mandates, etc. None noted.
c) Non-institutional regulations, polices, mandates, etc.: Peace Officer Standards and
Training (POST) has direct oversight over the Law Enforcement Academy training
standards. Dynamic nature of law requires bi-annual updates to course work for all
courses.
a. Requirements of employers: local law enforcement agencies have been
hiring our academy cadets who meet the minimum entry-level
requirements and have earned mandated certifications.
b. Developments in the field (current and former): New technologies,
especially in the area of criminal investigations, have required curriculum
updates. New criminal laws and arrest and control have also required
curriculum updates.
Other: A lack of professional development funding requires faculty to
maintain currency in the field and learn about new technologies on their
own. The lack of a CBA also inhibits faculty from obtaining stipends to
improve the program.
c. POST is the only regulatory entity pertaining to law enforcement training
standards.
d) Job Market
a. Law Enforcement Academy meets the needs of prospective employers
b. Academy prepares students for entry-level employment in law enforcement.

-- 3 of 8 --

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 Academy (Part 4)
text:
c. Development in the field –changing technologies and educational requirements
affect recruitment and retention for this job market.
2. Progress on Outcomes Assessment
a) SLOs evaluated bi-annually and evaluated for alignment with programmatic SLOs
b) None
c) Continual program and course assessment throughout next four years
3 . Progress on Outcomes Assessment
a) Summarize progress on SLO measures applied since last review: SLO’s are
formulated from P.O.S.T Training and Testing Specifications which are updated
prior to the start of an academy in an expanded course outline to ensure
coverage of state mandated material.
b) Program, course, instructional improvements made as a result of outcomes
assessment: All curriculum updated and approved by P.O.S.T. prior to start of
each academy.
c) Program assessment plan for next two years: Continuous monitoring of case law,
Industry standards and technology as well as mandated curriculum updates.
3. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators
a)
1. Certification rate 2018:
ADJ 2014 Module III 93.3%
ADJ 2516 Module II 91.67%
ADJ 2517 Module I 72.73%
2. Certification rate (two academies) 2019:
ADJ 2014 Module III 96.78%
ADJ 2516 Module II 96.55%
ADJ 2517 Module I 92.23%
3. Certification rate 2020:
ADJ 2014 Module III 90.32%
ADJ 2516 Module II 89.29%
ADJ 2517 Module I (Pending)
ADJ 2512 832 P.C. 100.00%
b) Course success rate: Out of the 86 students graduating our program 68 obtained
employment. Our program success for placing students in the workforce as law
enforcement officers is 79%. We currently have six students in the hiring process
and upon successful completion of the process will increase this number.
1. 2018, 8 students graduated, 8 students currently working in law enforcement
2. 2019, 1st academy, 28 graduated 21 currently working in law enforcement

-- 4 of 8 --

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 Academy (Part 7)
text:
4. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research
a) Certification Information
Students receiving a certificate Year
46 2018/2019
40 2019/2020
Industry certification or license Year
b) Based upon the above data tables, students are achieving certifications and degrees
at above-average rates
c) Evaluation of these measures indicate students have found gainful employment in
the field or earned a four-year degree in Criminal Justice.
5. Evaluation
a) Women are underrepresented in the program.
b) More online offerings and evening courses will improve student success in obtaining
A.S. Degree
c) Partnerships with local law enforcement agencies within the service area are robust.
d) Best practices are updated and implemented as the dynamic of the law requires.
e) Frustration in lack of facilities for the growth of the academic program and law
enforcement academy.
f) Staffing needs are currently not met because certain academy staff positions remain
unfilled (Director, Clerical)
g) Robust Advisory Commitment that meets bi-annually to discuss planning and
decision-making of the program; faculty participate in institutional shared
governance.
h) Faculty flex opportunities have met some professional development needs; however,
the changing law enforcement profession and dynamic nature of the law require
faculty, on their own time, to pursue professional development opportunities outside
their work schedules.
i) Law Enforcement Academy complies with the California Commission on POST; CTE
program reviews and course updates are completed every two years.
6. Vision
In the future we hope to have a dedicated training facility for our academic, police
academy, and advanced officer training programs. Because the Administration of
Justice Program has such a robust program, we would like to have enough room to
simultaneously offer courses and ensure future growth of the program. Additionally, we
would like to hire adequate staffing for the program and send instructors to conferences
and trainings to update their skills, will keep the program complaint with POST
standards. We would like to also facilitate multiple academies during the academic year
(day and evening academies). Further, scheduling additional online offerings will reach
more students.

-- 7 of 8 --

7. Progress on Prior Goals
a) Prior goals to establish a location on campus for the program to grow has
been resolved at this time. However, a dedicated facility would be optimal
for growth of program.
8. Four-Year Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal
